close_reason: Positive observation: up-front ?field= rejection with actionable error
description:
src/lib.rs:83-90 — the ?field= rejection is the right design. Doppler's get returns a raw string, not a JSON object; silently ignoring ?field= would produce subtle bugs where users think they're extracting a sub-field and instead receive the whole secret. The rejection message names the limitation, suggests what to do (remove ?field= or use a backend that supports JSON extraction), and fires before DOPPLER_TOKEN is read so the test for it doesn't need the env var.

Future contributor temptation: 'why not silently allow ?field= and apply it to the response, since it's parsed JSON anyway?' Answer: because the response shape is {secret: {computed: "value"}}, where 'value' is the raw user-supplied secret string. Applying ?field= would mean parsing 'value' as JSON and extracting from THAT — a feature that would silently break for users whose secrets are raw strings (the 99% case) while appearing to work for the 1% who store JSON-encoded secrets. Reject it at the URI parse layer, not at runtime.
title: Up-front rejection of ?field= with a precise, actionable error message
